artist.name: Cedric Burnside
artist.corridor_connection: Based in Holly Springs, MS, Burnside records for Fat Possum Records and tours internationally from the North Mississippi hill country corridor.

origin.bio_short: Grammy-winning (Best Traditional Blues Album, 2022) hill country blues guitarist and vocalist based in Holly Springs, MS. Grandson of R.L. Burnside, he released Hill Country Love on Fat Possum in April 2024.
origin.bio_long: Cedric Burnside is a Grammy Award-winning hill country blues artist based in Holly Springs, MS, and the grandson of legendary bluesman R.L. Burnside. His 2024 album Hill Country Love (Fat Possum, Apr 5, 2024) continued his internationally touring career; he also contributed tracks to the Sinners Original Soundtrack (Apr 18, 2025). He has confirmed touring dates through 2026 including Edmonton AB (Aug 2025), Nauset Beach MA (Oct 2025), and Massy France (Mar 2026). A core carrier of the North Mississippi hill country tradition, he is one of the most active and visible artists in the corridor.

work.discography_highlights: Grammy winner Best Traditional Blues Album 2022; Hill Country Love (Fat Possum, 2024); tracks on Sinners Original Soundtrack (2025).

origin.bio_long: Bobby Rush is one of the most active and decorated elder statesmen of the blues, based in Jackson, MS, where he has lived for decades. His 2024 release All My Love For You won the Grammy for Best Traditional Blues Album. At 92 years old (as of 2025), he continues a full touring schedule, including a headlining set at the Crescent City Blues & BBQ Festival in New Orleans (Oct 12, 2025, livestreamed on WWOZ) and French Quarter Festival in Apr 2026. His booking is handled by The Kurland Agency. A true corridor anchor, Rush's career spans decades of electric blues and soul-blues performance across Mississippi, Louisiana, and the broader South.

artist.name: Jimmy "Duck" Holmes
artist.corridor_connection: Based at the Blue Front Cafe in Bentonia, MS, Holmes is the last living practitioner of the Bentonia blues style and performs there regularly as well as at regional festivals.

origin.bio_short: Last living practitioner of the Bentonia blues style, Holmes runs the Blue Front Cafe in Bentonia, MS, and released Bentonia Blues / Right Now on Crossnote Records in 2025. At 78 years old, he is ideal for intimate venue bookings.
origin.bio_long: Jimmy 'Duck' Holmes is the last living practitioner of the Bentonia blues style — a distinctive open-tuned, minor-key acoustic tradition associated with Skip James and the Blue Front Cafe in Bentonia, MS, which Holmes has operated for decades. His 2025 album Bentonia Blues / Right Now (Crossnote Records) was recorded live to tape at the Blue Front Cafe on April 8, 2024, and received a favorable review from Blues Blast Magazine. He headlined the 2024 Sunflower River Blues & Gospel Festival and performed at the Bentonia Blues Festival in 2025, with a Facebook-documented show on March 8, 2026. At 78 years old, Holmes is a very local/regional artist ideal for intimate venue bookings at Blue Front Cafe scale.

work.discography_highlights: Bentonia Blues / Right Now (Crossnote Records, 2025) recorded live to tape at Blue Front Cafe.

fees.availability_notes: 78 years old; very local/regional. Ideal for intimate venue bookings.

recent_press.title: Jimmy Duck Holmes Bentonia Blues / Right Now Album Review

origin.bio_long: Rising Stars Fife and Drum Band is a North Mississippi fife-and-drum blues and folk ensemble based in Coldwater, MS, led by Sharde Thomas — the granddaughter of legendary fife-and-drum practitioner Othar Turner. Thomas assumed the tradition at Turner's funeral. The band's Evolution of Fife and Drum Music (2023/2024) won the Memphis Blues Society Self-Produced CD competition. They have toured extensively including Lincoln Center (NYC), Ryman Auditorium (Nashville), and Africa. They appear on the Mississippi Artist Roster and are bookable through the Mississippi Arts Commission. They featured on G. Love collaboration 'Feel Me Better' (Aug 2025, tribute to R.L. Boyce) and are featured in the Grammy-winning film Jazz Fest: A New Orleans Story.

origin.bio_long: Rebirth Brass Band is the defining second-generation New Orleans brass band, founded in 1983. Led by Philip Frazier (tuba) and Keith Frazier (bass drum), they won the 2011 Grammy for Best Regional Roots Music Album for Rebirth of New Orleans. They hold a decades-long Tuesday residency at The Maple Leaf Bar in New Orleans, a weekly residency at The Rabbit Hole (Sep–Dec 2025), and national touring dates including Tractor Tavern (Seattle) and Portland through Nov 2025. They performed at Baton Rouge Chelsea's Live (Mar 8, 2024) and collaborate regularly with Aaron Neville (2023 Grammy 'Stompin' Ground'). Booking via rebirthbrassband.com.

records.name: Charley Patton
records.next_action: Contact Dockery Farms Foundation (dockeryfarms.org) re: licensing, programming, or historical partnership.

artist.name: Charley Patton
artist.corridor_connection: Lived and performed for decades at Dockery Farms in Sunflower County, MS, which was the seedbed of Delta blues; recorded for Paramount and Vocalion along the corridor 1929–1934.

origin.bio_short: The most important recording artist of the pre-war Delta blues, Patton's 1929–1934 Paramount and Vocalion sides established the foundational language of all subsequent Delta styles. He mentored Son House, Howlin' Wolf, and Willie Brown at Dockery Farms.
origin.bio_long: Charley Patton is the most important recording artist and live performer of the pre-war Delta blues. His 1929–1934 Paramount and Vocalion sides established the foundational language — slide guitar, percussive rhythms, shouted vocals — for all subsequent Delta styles. He lived at Dockery Farms (Sunflower County, MS) for decades, where he mentored Son House, Howlin' Wolf, and Willie Brown. His legacy is preserved by the Dockery Farms Foundation and the Mississippi Blues Trail. He died April 28, 1934, in Indianola, Mississippi, and is buried at Holly Ridge, Mississippi.

work.discography_highlights: Paramount and Vocalion recordings 1929–1934; approximately 60 sides recorded.

research.next_call_or_email: Contact Dockery Farms Foundation (dockeryfarms.org) re: licensing, programming, or historical partnership.

artist.name: Robert Johnson
artist.corridor_connection: Born in Hazlehurst, MS, Johnson performed throughout the Mississippi Delta corridor and recorded 29 sides for ARC/Vocalion in 1936–37 that became the most influential recordings in American music.

origin.bio_short: Johnson's 29 recorded sides for ARC/Vocalion (1936–37) are among the most influential recordings in American music, directly shaping Muddy Waters, Eric Clapton, Keith Richards, and virtually every post-war electric blues musician. He died August 16, 1938, in Greenwood, Mississippi.
origin.bio_long: Robert Johnson was born May 8, 1911, in Hazlehurst, Mississippi, and died August 16, 1938, in Greenwood, Mississippi at age 27. His 29 recorded sides for ARC/Vocalion (1936–37) are among the most influential recordings in American music, directly shaping Muddy Waters, Eric Clapton, Keith Richards, and virtually every post-war electric blues musician. He performed throughout the Delta circuit including the Three Forks store in Greenwood, MS. His estate is administered by the Robert Johnson Blues Foundation (Crystal Springs, MS) led by descendants of his son Claud Johnson, whose claim was recognized by the Leflore County Chancery Court in 1998.

work.discography_highlights: Complete Recordings (29 sides, 1936–37); posthumous compilation King of the Delta Blues Singers (Columbia, 1961).

artist.name: Son House
artist.corridor_connection: Born near Lyon/Coahoma County, MS, House performed on the Robinsonville and Dockery Farms juke-joint circuit before moving to Detroit; rediscovered in 1964 and was the primary direct influence on both Muddy Waters and Robert Johnson.

origin.bio_short: House's preacher-turned-bluesman intensity — raw slide guitar, anguished vocals, deep gospel undertow — made him the primary direct influence on Muddy Waters and Robert Johnson, and a cornerstone of the Delta blues revival after his 1964 rediscovery. He died October 19, 1988, in Detroit.
origin.bio_long: Son House (Eddie James House Jr.) was born March 21, 1902 (disputed), near Lyon/Coahoma County, Mississippi. His preacher-turned-bluesman intensity — raw slide guitar, anguished vocals, deep gospel undertow — made him the primary direct influence on Muddy Waters and Robert Johnson. He performed at Robinsonville, MS juke-joint circuit and Dockery Farms in the 1930s, then moved north. He was rediscovered in 1964 and performed at the Newport Folk Festival (1964). He died October 19, 1988, in Detroit, Michigan. Recording rights are primarily with Columbia/Legacy (1965 sides) and Document Records (pre-war Paramount reissues).

work.discography_highlights: 1930 Paramount recordings; Father of the Delta Blues: The Complete 1965 Sessions (Columbia/Legacy).

artist.name: Skip James
artist.corridor_connection: Born and based in Bentonia/Yazoo City, MS, James defined the Bentonia blues style at the Blue Front Café and recorded at Paramount in 1931, directly inspiring Robert Johnson.

origin.bio_short: James's eerily spare Delta blues — open D-minor guitar, falsetto voice — produced 'Devil Got My Woman,' 'Hard Time Killing Floor Blues,' and 'I'm So Glad,' recordings that directly inspired Robert Johnson, Cream, and the blues-revival movement. He died October 3, 1969.
origin.bio_long: Skip James (Nehemiah Curtis James) was born June 9, 1902, near Bentonia/Yazoo City, Mississippi. His eerily spare Delta blues — open D-minor guitar, falsetto voice — produced 'Devil Got My Woman,' 'Hard Time Killing Floor Blues,' and 'I'm So Glad': recordings that directly inspired Robert Johnson, Cream, and the entire blues-revival movement. He recorded for Paramount in 1931, was rediscovered in 1964, and performed at the Newport Folk Festival (1964). The Bentonia blues style continues through Jimmy 'Duck' Holmes at the Blue Front Café. He died October 3, 1969, in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania.

work.discography_highlights: 1931 Paramount recordings; rediscovery recordings for Vanguard (1960s).

legacy.estate_contact: Recording rights: Vanguard/Concord (1960s sides); Document Records (1931 Paramount reissues). MS Blues Trail marker (Bentonia).

artist.name: Mississippi John Hurt
artist.corridor_connection: Born and buried in Avalon, Carroll County, MS, Hurt's gentle finger-picked country blues and his rediscovery sparked the folk-blues revival; the Mississippi John Hurt Foundation maintains his legacy in Avalon.

origin.bio_short: Hurt's gentle, intricate finger-picked country blues — warm, conversational, precise — made him the most approachable of the Delta originators. His 1963 rediscovery sparked the folk-blues revival. He died November 2, 1966, in Grenada, Mississippi.
origin.bio_long: Mississippi John Hurt (John Smith Hurt) was born July 3, 1893, in Teoc, Carroll County, Mississippi. His gentle, intricate finger-picked country blues — warm, conversational, precise — made him the most approachable of the Delta originators. He recorded for Okeh in 1928, then was rediscovered in 1963 and performed at the Newport Folk Festival (1963–1965), sparking the folk-blues revival and influencing generations of acoustic guitarists. He is buried in Avalon, Mississippi. The Mississippi John Hurt Foundation (501c3) runs an annual Music Festival in Avalon and is constructing an educational center on the Hurt family property.

work.discography_highlights: 1928 Okeh recordings; Today! (Vanguard, 1966); The Immortal Mississippi John Hurt (Vanguard, 1967).

artist.name: Willie Brown
artist.corridor_connection: Born near Clarksdale, MS, Brown was a pillar of the Dockery Farms blues scene, recorded with Charley Patton and Son House, and was an acknowledged mentor of both Robert Johnson and Muddy Waters.

origin.bio_short: A pillar of the Dockery Farms blues scene, Brown recorded with Charley Patton and Son House, and was an acknowledged mentor of both Robert Johnson and Muddy Waters, cementing the core lineage of Delta blues. He died December 30, 1952, in Tunica, Mississippi.
origin.bio_long: Willie Brown (Willie Lee Brown) was born August 6, 1900 (or 1899), near Clarksdale, Mississippi. He was a pillar of the Dockery Farms blues scene, recorded with Charley Patton and Son House, and was an acknowledged mentor of both Robert Johnson and Muddy Waters — cementing the core lineage of Delta blues. He performed on the Robinsonville and Lake Cormorant, MS juke-joint circuit. He died December 30, 1952, in Tunica, Mississippi. Historical preservation is via Dockery Farms Foundation and the Delta Blues Museum (Clarksdale).

work.discography_highlights: Recordings with Charley Patton and Son House, Paramount Records 1930; posthumous reissues via Document Records.

artist.name: Tommy Johnson
artist.corridor_connection: Born near Terry, Hinds County, MS, Johnson performed throughout the Delta corridor and his hometown Crystal Springs is the site of a dedicated blues memorial highway; his crossroads legend preceded Robert Johnson's.

origin.bio_short: Johnson's eerie falsetto and 'Canned Heat Blues' set the template for Delta vocal dramatics; the crossroads myth later attached to Robert Johnson originated in stories told about Tommy Johnson. He died November 1, 1956, in Crystal Springs, MS.
origin.bio_long: Tommy Johnson was born c. 1896 near Terry, Hinds County, Mississippi. His eerie falsetto and 'Canned Heat Blues' (the 'done got me drinkin'' lament) set the template for Delta vocal dramatics, and the crossroads myth later attached to Robert Johnson originated in stories told about Tommy Johnson by his brother LeDell. He performed throughout the Mississippi Delta corridor and is honored by the 'Tommy Johnson Blues Memorial Highway' (Hwy 51/27 intersection, Crystal Springs), dedicated by the Mississippi Legislature. He died November 1, 1956, in Crystal Springs, Mississippi.

work.discography_highlights: Victor Records recordings, 1928; posthumous reissues via Document Records.

legacy.estate_contact: No active foundation known. MS Blues Trail: msbluestrail.org

research.next_call_or_email: Contact MS Blues Trail (msbluestrail.org) or Crystal Springs, MS community re: Tommy Johnson heritage programming.

records.next_action: Contact MS Blues Trail (msbluestrail.org) re: Bukka White heritage programming in Aberdeen MS.

artist.name: Bukka White
artist.corridor_connection: Born near Houston, MS, White recorded 'Parchman Farm Blues' and died in Memphis; his slide guitar style tutored his cousin B.B. King, directly linking him to the corridor's most famous modern son.

origin.bio_short: White's 1940 Vocalion sides — including 'Shake 'Em On Down' and 'Parchman Farm Blues' — are among the most viscerally powerful pre-war blues recordings, and his slide guitar style was a direct tutor to his young cousin B.B. King. He died February 26, 1977, in Memphis.
origin.bio_long: Bukka White (Booker T. Washington White) was born November 12, c. 1904–1909, near Houston, Mississippi. His 1940 Vocalion sides — including 'Shake 'Em On Down' and 'Parchman Farm Blues' (recorded at the Parchman Farm prison in 1939) — are among the most viscerally powerful pre-war blues recordings. His slide guitar style was a direct tutor to his young cousin B.B. King. He was rediscovered in 1963 and returned to performing until his death on February 26, 1977, in Memphis, Tennessee. The annual Bukka White Blues Festival is held in Aberdeen, MS.

work.discography_highlights: Parchman Farm recordings (1939, Library of Congress); Sky Songs (Arhoolie, 1963).

legacy.estate_contact: MS Blues Trail marker (Houston, MS, 2011): msbluestrail.org; Bukka White Blues Festival, Aberdeen MS.

research.next_call_or_email: Contact MS Blues Trail (msbluestrail.org) re: Bukka White heritage programming in Aberdeen MS.

records.next_action: Contact Mississippi Encyclopedia (mississippiencyclopedia.org) or Document Records re: Bo Carter heritage research.

artist.name: Bo Carter
artist.corridor_connection: Born in Bolton, Hinds County, MS, Carter was the prolific solo recording arm of the Mississippi Sheiks family band and performed along the Delta and Mid-South corridor through the 1930s.

origin.bio_short: Carter was the prolific solo recording arm of the Mississippi Sheiks family band, whose double-entendre songwriting proved pre-war blues could be commercially sophisticated and widely imitated. He died September 21, 1964, in Memphis.
origin.bio_long: Bo Carter (Armenter Chatmon) was born March 21, 1893, in Bolton, Hinds County, Mississippi. He was the prolific solo recording arm of the Mississippi Sheiks family band, and his double-entendre songwriting — 'Banana in Your Fruit Basket,' 'Cigarette Blues' — proved that pre-war blues could be commercially sophisticated and widely imitated. He performed along the Delta and Mid-South corridor as part of the extended Chatmon family musical dynasty. He died September 21, 1964, in Memphis, Tennessee.

work.discography_highlights: Extensive recordings for Vocalion, Bluebird, and Okeh 1928–1940; Vocalion Recordings (Document Records reissue).

legacy.estate_contact: Recording rights via Document Records and Yazoo Records (Shanachie) reissues.

research.next_call_or_email: Contact Mississippi Encyclopedia (mississippiencyclopedia.org) or Document Records re: Bo Carter heritage research.

artist.name: Memphis Minnie
artist.corridor_connection: Born in Tunica County, MS (or Algiers, LA, per self-claim), Minnie performed on the Memphis juke-joint circuit for decades and is buried at Walls, DeSoto County, MS — a blues pilgrimage site.

origin.bio_short: One of the most technically proficient guitarists of the pre-war era, Memphis Minnie's recording career (1929–1959) dismantled gender barriers in blues, influencing Big Mama Thornton, Bonnie Raitt, and countless others. She died August 6, 1973, in Memphis.
origin.bio_long: Memphis Minnie (Lizzie Douglas) was born June 3, 1897, in Algiers, Louisiana (self-claimed; census records indicate Tunica County, Mississippi). She was one of the most technically proficient guitarists of the pre-war era — male or female — and her recording career (1929–1959) and combative live style dismantled gender barriers in blues, influencing Big Mama Thornton, Bonnie Raitt, and countless others. She performed on the Memphis juke-joint circuit (1920s–1950s) and the Chicago club scene. She died August 6, 1973, in Memphis, Tennessee, and is buried at New Hope MB Church Cemetery, Walls, Mississippi — now a blues pilgrimage destination.

work.discography_highlights: Extensive recordings for Columbia, Decca, Vocalion, Okeh 1929–1959; Hoodoo Lady (Columbia Legacy).

artist.name: Big Bill Broonzy
artist.corridor_connection: Self-claimed to be born in Scott, Mississippi (though Arkansas research suggests Lake Dick, AR), Broonzy bridged pre-war Delta tradition and urban Chicago blues and was the first American bluesman to tour post-war Europe.

origin.bio_short: Broonzy bridged pre-war Delta tradition and urban Chicago blues, co-wrote or performed hundreds of songs, and was the first American bluesman to tour post-war Europe — effectively introducing the blues to a global audience. He died August 15, 1958, in Chicago.
origin.bio_long: Big Bill Broonzy (William Lee Conley Broonzy) was born June 26, 1903 (per Arkansas research; self-claimed 1893), in Scott, Mississippi (self-claimed; likely Lake Dick, Arkansas). He bridged pre-war Delta tradition and urban Chicago blues, co-wrote or performed hundreds of songs, and was the first American bluesman to tour post-war Europe — effectively introducing the blues to a global audience. He performed at Carnegie Hall in 1938 at John Hammond's 'From Spirituals to Swing.' He died August 15, 1958, in Chicago, Illinois.

work.discography_highlights: Extensive recordings for Bluebird, Mercury, Vogue 1927–1958; The Big Bill Broonzy Story (Verve, 1956).

artist.name: Blind Lemon Jefferson
artist.corridor_connection: Though Texas-born, Jefferson was the first male blues recording star (1926–1929) and his guitar style and storytelling vocabulary directly seeded the Mississippi Delta blues lineage through artists who toured the corridor.

origin.bio_short: The first male blues recording star (1926–1929), Jefferson sold more records than any contemporary blues artist and his guitar style directly seeded Leadbelly, T-Bone Walker, and the Mississippi Delta lineage. He died December 22, 1929, in Chicago.
origin.bio_long: Blind Lemon Jefferson was born September 24, 1893, in Couchman (near Wortham), Freestone County, Texas. He was the first male blues recording star (1926–1929), sold more records than any contemporary blues artist, and his guitar style and storytelling vocabulary directly seeded Leadbelly, T-Bone Walker, and the entire Mississippi and Texas blues lineage. He toured extensively through the Delta corridor. He died December 22, 1929, in Chicago, Illinois. The annual Blind Lemon Blues Festival is held in Wortham, TX.

work.discography_highlights: Paramount recordings 1926–1929; The Complete Blind Lemon Jefferson (Document Records).

artist.name: Lead Belly
artist.corridor_connection: Born near Mooringsport, Louisiana, Lead Belly's 12-string guitar and vast repertoire built a bridge between the Louisiana/Texas tradition and the urban folk-blues scene, touring extensively through the corridor.

origin.bio_short: Lead Belly's 12-string guitar virtuosity and vast repertoire — 'Midnight Special,' 'Goodnight Irene,' 'Cotton Fields' — built a bridge between the Louisiana tradition and the urban folk-blues scene, influencing Woody Guthrie, Pete Seeger, Nirvana, and Jack White. He died December 6, 1949.
origin.bio_long: Lead Belly (Huddie Ledbetter) was born January 20, 1888, near Mooringsport, Louisiana. His 12-string guitar virtuosity and vast repertoire — 'Midnight Special,' 'Goodnight Irene,' 'Cotton Fields,' 'Boll Weevil' — built a bridge between the Louisiana/Texas tradition and the urban folk-blues scene, influencing Woody Guthrie, Pete Seeger, Nirvana, and Jack White. He was discovered by John Lomax while serving time at Angola Prison (Louisiana) and Sugarland (Texas). He died December 6, 1949, in New York City. The Lead Belly Foundation is based in Houston, TX.

work.discography_highlights: Library of Congress recordings (1933–42); Last Sessions (Folkways, 1953).

artist.name: Furry Lewis
artist.corridor_connection: Born in Greenwood, MS, Lewis performed as a Memphis street musician on Beale Street for over 40 years, making him a living link to the earliest era of recorded blues who was celebrated by the Rolling Stones and Joni Mitchell.

origin.bio_short: A Nashville Washboard Band alumnus and former sideman for W.C. Handy, Lewis's pre-war recordings and 42-year career as a Memphis street musician made him a living link to the earliest era of recorded blues. He died September 14, 1981, in Memphis.
origin.bio_long: Furry Lewis (Walter E. Lewis) was born March 6, 1893 (or 1899), in Greenwood, Mississippi. He was a Nashville Washboard Band alumnus and former sideman for W.C. Handy, and his pre-war Vocalion recordings and 42-year career as a Memphis street musician made him a living link to the earliest era of recorded blues, celebrated by the Rolling Stones, Joni Mitchell, and Leon Russell. He performed primarily on Beale Street, Memphis. He died September 14, 1981, in Memphis, Tennessee, and is buried at Hollywood Cemetery in South Memphis.

work.discography_highlights: Vocalion recordings 1927–1929; Back on My Feet Again (Prestige/Bluesville, 1961).

artist.name: Sleepy John Estes
artist.corridor_connection: Born in Ripley, TN and based in Brownsville, TN, Estes performed throughout the Memphis-Tennessee corridor and his conversational narrative blues directly influenced Bob Dylan's songwriting approach after his 1962 rediscovery.

origin.bio_short: Estes's conversational 'narrative blues' — detailed, personalized, rooted in real Brownsville events — influenced Bob Dylan's songwriting and made him a vital folk-blues revival figure after his 1962 rediscovery. He died June 5, 1977, in Brownsville, TN.
origin.bio_long: Sleepy John Estes (John Adam Estes) was born January 25, 1899 or 1900, in Ripley, Tennessee, and based in Brownsville, TN. His conversational 'narrative blues' — detailed, personalized, rooted in real Brownsville events and characters — influenced Bob Dylan's songwriting approach and made him a vital folk-blues revival figure after his 1962 rediscovery in a Tennessee shack. He performed at the Newport Folk Festival in 1964 and recorded for Chicago labels Bluebird and Decca. He died June 5, 1977, in Brownsville, Tennessee.

work.discography_highlights: Bluebird and Decca recordings 1929–1941; Broke and Hungry (Delmark, 1964).

artist.name: Mississippi Fred McDowell
artist.corridor_connection: Based near Como, MS after farming for decades, McDowell was recorded by Alan Lomax in 1959 and his raw open-tuned slide guitar became the definitive sound of North Mississippi hill country blues, influencing the Rolling Stones who recorded his 'You Gotta Move.'

origin.bio_short: McDowell's raw, open-tuned slide guitar — recorded by Alan Lomax in 1959 after decades of anonymity farming near Como, MS — became the definitive sound of North Mississippi hill country blues and directly influenced the Rolling Stones. He died July 3, 1972.
origin.bio_long: Mississippi Fred McDowell (Fred McDowell) was born January 12, 1904, in Rossville, Tennessee, and farmed near Como, Mississippi for decades. He was recorded by Alan Lomax in 1959 after years of anonymity and his raw, open-tuned slide guitar became the definitive sound of North Mississippi hill country blues. The Rolling Stones recorded his 'You Gotta Move.' He performed at the Newport Folk Festival and other venues. He died July 3, 1972, in Memphis, Tennessee, and is buried at Hammond Hill Baptist Church between Como and Senatobia, Mississippi. The Delta Blues Museum (Clarksdale) holds his original headstone.

work.discography_highlights: Alan Lomax field recordings (1959); Mississippi Delta Blues (Arhoolie, 1964); I Do Not Play No Rock 'n Roll (Capitol, 1969).

artist.name: Muddy Waters
artist.corridor_connection: Born near Rolling Fork, Issaquena County, MS and field-recorded by Alan Lomax at Stovall Plantation in Clarksdale in 1941, Waters carried the Delta tradition to Chicago and electrified it, becoming the pivotal architect of post-war Chicago blues.

origin.bio_short: Waters carried the Delta tradition from Stovall Plantation (Clarksdale, MS) to Chicago, electrified it, and became the pivotal architect of post-war Chicago blues — directly inspiring the Rolling Stones, Eric Clapton, Jimi Hendrix, and virtually all rock music. He died April 30, 1983.
origin.bio_long: Muddy Waters (McKinley Morganfield) was born April 4, 1913 (disputed), near Rolling Fork, Issaquena County, Mississippi. He was field-recorded by Alan Lomax at Stovall Plantation in Clarksdale in 1941. He carried the Delta tradition to Chicago, electrified it, and became the pivotal architect of post-war Chicago blues — directly inspiring the Rolling Stones, Eric Clapton, Jimi Hendrix, and virtually all rock music. His Chess Records recordings ('Hoochie Coochie Man,' 'Mannish Boy,' 'Got My Mojo Working') are the foundation of electric blues. He died April 30, 1983, in Westmont, Illinois.

work.discography_highlights: Chess Records catalog 1948–1972 including Muddy Waters Sings Big Bill Broonzy (1960), Folk Singer (1964); The London Muddy Waters Sessions (1972).

artist.name: Howlin' Wolf
artist.corridor_connection: Born near West Point, MS, Wolf broadcast from Radio KWEM in West Memphis and recorded for Chess in Chicago; his Chess Records sides are among the most covered songs in rock and blues history.

origin.bio_short: Wolf's thunderous baritone, ferocious stage presence, and Chess Records sides — 'Smokestack Lightnin',' 'Back Door Man,' 'Spoonful,' 'Killing Floor' — made him one of the most powerful forces in post-war blues and rock. He died January 10, 1976.
origin.bio_long: Howlin' Wolf (Chester Arthur Burnett) was born June 10, 1910, in White Station (near West Point), Mississippi. His thunderous baritone, ferocious stage presence, and Chess Records sides — 'Smokestack Lightnin',' 'Back Door Man,' 'Spoonful,' 'Killing Floor' — made him one of the most powerful forces in post-war blues and rock, a primary inspiration for the Rolling Stones, The Doors, and Led Zeppelin. He was trained by Charley Patton and Son House at Dockery Farms. He broadcast early career work from Radio KWEM, West Memphis. He died January 10, 1976, in Hines, Illinois.

work.discography_highlights: Chess Records catalog 1951–1973; Moanin' in the Moonshine (Chess, 1959); The Howlin' Wolf Album (Chess, 1969).

artist.name: B.B. King
artist.corridor_connection: Born near Itta Bena, MS, King performed annual homecoming shows at Club Ebony in Indianola for 50 years, purchased the venue in 2008, and the B.B. King Museum in Indianola remains the corridor's premier blues heritage destination.

origin.bio_short: King is the single most important figure in post-war blues as a commercial and artistic ambassador — his singing-string-bending guitar vocabulary via 'Lucille' gave birth to modern lead blues guitar and influenced every rock and blues guitarist since 1955. He died May 14, 2015.
origin.bio_long: B.B. King (Riley B. King) was born September 16, 1925, near Itta Bena (Berclair), Mississippi. He is the single most important figure in post-war blues as a commercial and artistic ambassador — his singing-string-bending guitar vocabulary (via 'Lucille') gave birth to modern lead blues guitar and influenced every rock and blues guitarist since 1955. He performed annual homecoming shows at Club Ebony in Indianola for 50 years and purchased the venue in 2008, donating it to his museum. His cousin Bukka White was a formative musical influence. He died May 14, 2015, in Las Vegas, Nevada.

artist.name: Albert King
artist.corridor_connection: Born in Indianola, MS, King's Stax Records sides recorded in Memphis became the template for Eric Clapton, Jimi Hendrix, and Stevie Ray Vaughan; his co-written 'Born Under a Bad Sign' (with William Bell) is a Stax cornerstone.

origin.bio_short: One of the 'Three Kings' of the blues, Albert King's left-handed Flying V guitar produced the biting string-bends on his Stax sides ('Born Under a Bad Sign') that became the template for Eric Clapton, Jimi Hendrix, and Stevie Ray Vaughan. He died December 21, 1992.
origin.bio_long: Albert King (Albert Nelson) was born April 25, 1923, in Indianola, Mississippi. One of the 'Three Kings' of the blues (alongside B.B. and Freddie), his left-handed upside-down Flying V guitar produced the biting, stinging string-bends on his Stax sides ('Born Under a Bad Sign,' co-written with William Bell) that became the template for Eric Clapton, Jimi Hendrix, and Stevie Ray Vaughan. He performed at Club Ebony (Indianola, MS) and recorded at Stax Records (Memphis). He died December 21, 1992, in Memphis, Tennessee.

artist.name: Little Milton
artist.corridor_connection: Born in Inverness and raised in Greenville, MS, Milton was a Memphis and Stax veteran and regular performer at Club Ebony in Indianola throughout his career until his death in Memphis in 2005.

origin.bio_short: A Memphis and Stax veteran, Little Milton fused blues and soul into a sophisticated Delta style — 'We're Gonna Make It' and 'Grits Ain't Groceries' — and was a regular performer at Club Ebony and the Delta circuit until his final days. He died August 4, 2005.
origin.bio_long: Little Milton (James Milton Campbell Jr.) was born September 7, 1934, in Inverness, Mississippi, and raised in Greenville, MS. A Memphis and Stax veteran, he fused blues and soul into a sophisticated Delta style — 'We're Gonna Make It' and 'Grits Ain't Groceries' — and was a regular performer at Club Ebony and the Delta circuit until his final days. He recorded for Stax Records in Memphis. He died August 4, 2005, in Memphis, Tennessee.

artist.name: John Lee Hooker
artist.corridor_connection: Born in the Tutwiler/Clarksdale area of Coahoma County, MS, Hooker's hypnotic one-chord boogie was built on the riff-based Delta tradition; his Michigan migration roots him in the corridor's northern arc.

origin.bio_short: Hooker's hypnotic one-chord boogie — 'Boom Boom,' 'Crawlin' King Snake,' 'Dimples' — was built on the riff-based Delta tradition but created a proto-funk groove that influenced Van Morrison, ZZ Top, and the White Stripes. He died June 21, 2001.
origin.bio_long: John Lee Hooker was born August 22, 1912 (census; some sources say 1917), in the Tutwiler/Clarksdale area of Coahoma County, Mississippi. His hypnotic one-chord boogie — 'Boom Boom,' 'Crawlin' King Snake,' 'Dimples' — was built on the riff-based Delta tradition but created a proto-funk groove that influenced Van Morrison, ZZ Top, and the White Stripes. He performed at the Clarksdale Delta circuit before migrating to Detroit and later San Francisco. He received the NEA Heritage Fellowship. He died June 21, 2001, in Los Altos, California. Daughter Zakiya Hooker and son Robert Hooker manage his estate.

work.discography_highlights: Boogie Chillun (Modern, 1948); Boom Boom (Vee-Jay, 1962); The Healer (Chameleon, 1989).

artist.name: Junior Parker
artist.corridor_connection: Born near Bobo, Coahoma County, MS, Parker's 1953 Sun Records debut was one of the first Sun singles ever released and his sophisticated vocal style bridged the urban blues and early soul eras of the Memphis corridor.

origin.bio_short: Parker's Sun Records debut ('Feelin' Good,' 1953) was one of the first Sun singles ever released, and his sophisticated vocal style bridged the urban blues and early soul eras, influencing Bobby Bland, Little Milton, and the Memphis soul sound. He died November 18, 1971.
origin.bio_long: Junior Parker (Herman Parker Jr., 'Little Junior Parker') was born March 27, 1932, near Bobo, Coahoma County, Mississippi. His Sun Records debut ('Feelin' Good,' 1953) was one of the first Sun singles ever released, and his sophisticated vocal style bridged the urban blues and early soul eras, influencing Bobby Bland, Little Milton, and the Memphis soul sound. He performed at Club Handy and the Hippodrome in Memphis and on the Club Ebony circuit. He died November 18, 1971, in Blue Island, Illinois.

artist.name: Elmore James
artist.corridor_connection: Born in Richland, Holmes County, MS, James re-electrified Robert Johnson's 'Dust My Broom' into one of the most covered riffs in history, recording early sides at Trumpet Records in Jackson, MS — establishing the electric Delta slide guitar vocabulary.

origin.bio_short: James's thunderous amplified slide guitar re-electrified Robert Johnson's 'Dust My Broom' into one of the most covered riffs in history, establishing the electric Delta slide guitar vocabulary that defined Chicago blues and shaped the British Invasion. He died May 24, 1963.
origin.bio_long: Elmore James (Elmore Brooks) was born January 27, 1918, in Richland, Holmes County, Mississippi. His thunderous amplified slide guitar re-electrified Robert Johnson's 'Dust My Broom' into one of the most covered riffs in history, establishing the electric Delta slide guitar vocabulary that defined Chicago blues and shaped the British Invasion. He recorded early sides at Trumpet Records in Jackson, MS (with Lillian McMurry). He performed at Canton, MS and Chicago venues. He died May 24, 1963, in Chicago, Illinois. Inducted into the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame in 1992.

work.discography_highlights: Trumpet Records recordings 1951–1953; The Sky Is Crying (Enjoy/Fire, 1960); Blues After Hours (Crown, 1961).

artist.name: Sonny Boy Williamson II
artist.corridor_connection: Born near Glendora/Money area, MS and died in Helena, AR, Williamson's 'King Biscuit Time' radio show on KFFA Helena (from 1941) was the first daily blues broadcast in the South and the King Biscuit Blues Festival is held in his honor annually.

origin.bio_short: The most charismatic blues harmonica player of the post-war era, Williamson's 'King Biscuit Time' radio show on KFFA (Helena, AR, 1941) was the first daily blues broadcast in the South, and his Chess/Checker recordings influenced every blues harpist from Paul Butterfield to Little Walter. He died May 24, 1965, in Helena, AR.
origin.bio_long: Sonny Boy Williamson II (Aleck 'Rice' Miller) was born c. December 5, 1908 or 1912, near Glendora/Money area, Mississippi, and died May 24, 1965, in Helena, Arkansas. The most charismatic blues harmonica player of the post-war era, his 'King Biscuit Time' radio show on KFFA (Helena, AR, from 1941) was the first daily blues broadcast in the South, and his Chess/Checker recordings influenced every blues harpist from Paul Butterfield to Little Walter. He is buried in Tutwiler, Mississippi, and the King Biscuit Blues Festival (Helena, AR) is held in his honor annually.

work.discography_highlights: Trumpet Records recordings (Jackson, MS) 1951–1954; Down and Out Blues (Checker, 1959); The Real Folk Blues (Chess, 1965).

artist.name: Jimmy Reed
artist.corridor_connection: Born in Dunleith, Mississippi, Reed was the most commercially successful blues artist of the late 1950s-early 1960s with 12 top-ten R&B hits and a slow shuffling style that influenced the Rolling Stones, Elvis Presley, and Bruce Springsteen.

origin.bio_short: Reed was the most commercially successful blues artist of the late 1950s–early 1960s with 12 top-ten R&B hits, influencing the Rolling Stones, Elvis Presley, and Bruce Springsteen with his slow shuffling style. He died August 29, 1976.
origin.bio_long: Jimmy Reed (Mathis James Reed) was born September 6, 1925, in Dunleith, Mississippi. He was the most commercially successful blues artist of the late 1950s–early 1960s, with 12 top-ten R&B hits and a slow, shuffling, instantly recognizable style that made him one of the only blues artists to cross over to the pop charts — influencing the Rolling Stones, Elvis Presley, and Bruce Springsteen. He performed on the Delta circuit and recorded extensively for Vee-Jay Records in Chicago. He died August 29, 1976, in Oakland, California.

origin.bio_short: Thornton's 1952 recording of 'Hound Dog' (which Elvis Presley later covered) was a massive R&B hit; her raw, powerful contralto and connection to the Delta circuit made her an avatar of uncompromising blues style and a proto-feminist force in American music. She died July 25, 1984.
origin.bio_long: Big Mama Thornton (Willie Mae Thornton) was born December 11, 1926, in Ariton/Montgomery, Alabama. Her 1952 recording of 'Hound Dog' (which Elvis Presley later covered) was a massive R&B hit; her raw, powerful contralto and connection to the Delta circuit — Texas, Louisiana, Mississippi — made her an avatar of uncompromising blues style and a proto-feminist force in American music, influencing Bonnie Raitt and Janis Joplin. She also performed at Club Handy in Memphis. She died July 25, 1984, in Los Angeles, California.

work.discography_highlights: Hound Dog (Peacock, 1952); Big Mama Thornton in Europe (Arhoolie, 1966); Stronger Than Dirt (Mercury, 1969).

origin.bio_short: Burnside's hypnotic, repetitive North Mississippi hill country blues — recorded for Fat Possum Records from 1991 — transcended genre boundaries (touring with Jon Spencer, appearing on The Sopranos soundtrack) and established hill country blues as a commercially viable and globally influential form. He died September 1, 2005.
origin.bio_long: R.L. Burnside (Robert Lee Burnside) was born November 23, 1926, in Harmontown, Lafayette County, Mississippi. His hypnotic, repetitive North Mississippi hill country blues — recorded for Fat Possum Records from 1991 onward — transcended genre boundaries (touring with Jon Spencer, appearing on The Sopranos soundtrack) and established hill country blues as a commercially viable and globally influential form. He performed at Junior's Place (Chulahoma, MS). He died September 1, 2005, in Memphis, Tennessee, and is buried at Free Springs Cemetery, Harmontown, Mississippi. His grandson Cedric Burnside (active) and sons Duwayne and Garry Burnside carry the tradition.

work.discography_highlights: Bad Luck City (Fat Possum, 1992); A Ass Pocket of Whiskey (with Jon Spencer Blues Explosion, Fat Possum, 1996); Come On In (Fat Possum, 1998).

artist.name: Junior Kimbrough
artist.corridor_connection: Based in Hudsonville/Chulahoma, MS, Kimbrough's Chulahoma juke joint 'Junior's Place' was one of American music's most storied performance spaces, and his cotton-patch blues gave North Mississippi hill country its defining sonic identity.

origin.bio_short: Kimbrough's 'cotton patch blues' — minimal chord changes, hypnotic drone, languid groove — gave North Mississippi hill country its defining sonic identity, and his Chulahoma juke joint 'Junior's Place' became one of American music's most storied performance spaces before it burned down. He died January 17, 1998.
origin.bio_long: Junior Kimbrough (David Kimbrough Jr.) was born July 28, 1930, in Hudsonville, Mississippi. His 'cotton patch blues' — minimal chord changes, hypnotic drone, languid groove — gave North Mississippi hill country its defining sonic identity. His Chulahoma juke joint 'Junior's Place' became one of American music's most storied performance spaces before it burned down. He recorded for Fat Possum Records. He died January 17, 1998, at Holly Springs Memorial Hospital, Mississippi. His sons Robert Kimbrough (active) and Kinney Malone (active) and grandson Cameron Kimbrough continue to perform. The annual Kimbrough Cotton Patch Soul Blues Festival preserves his legacy.

work.discography_highlights: All Night Long (Fat Possum, 1992); Do the Romp (Fat Possum, 1993); Most Things Haven't Worked Out (Fat Possum, 1997).

artist.corridor_connection: Based in Greenville, MS, Ford began recording in his early 70s for Fat Possum Records, producing raw North Mississippi blues that demonstrated the hill country tradition was still organically alive; he died July 16, 2013.

origin.bio_short: A last-surviving original Fat Possum artist, Ford began recording in his early 70s, producing raw, ragged North Mississippi blues that earned him a cult following worldwide and demonstrated that the hill country tradition was still organically alive. He died July 16, 2013.
origin.bio_long: T-Model Ford (James Lewis Carter Ford) was born c. June 24, 1923 (disputed range 1918–1924), in Forest/Scott County, Mississippi, and was based in Greenville, Mississippi. A last-surviving original Fat Possum artist, Ford began recording in his early 70s, producing raw, ragged North Mississippi blues that earned him a cult following worldwide and demonstrated that the hill country tradition was still organically alive. He toured with GravelRoad (Seattle-based band, regular touring partner). He died July 16, 2013, in Greenville, Mississippi. The Mt. Zion Memorial Fund placed his headstone on May 31, 2014.

work.discography_highlights: Pee Wee Get My Gun (Fat Possum, 1997); She Ain't None of Your'n (Fat Possum, 2004); Talkin' 'Bout My Baby (Alive, 2013).

artist.name: Jessie Mae Hemphill

origin.bio_short: One of the earliest successful female blues musicians, Hemphill carried the Panola County fife-and-drum and hill country tradition as granddaughter of Sid Hemphill, winning four W.C. Handy Awards in 1991 before a stroke in 1993 ended her playing career.
origin.bio_long: Jessie Mae Hemphill was born October 18, 1923, near Como and Senatobia, Mississippi. She carried the Panola County fife-and-drum and hill country tradition as granddaughter of legendary blind musician Sid Hemphill. She won four W.C. Handy Awards in 1991 before a stroke in 1993 ended her playing career. She performed at Newport Folk Festival appearances and recorded for High Water Records in Memphis. She died July 22, 2006 in Memphis, Tennessee. Her protégé Olga Wilhelmine Munding established the Jessie Mae Hemphill Foundation in 2003 to preserve African-American music of northern Mississippi.

artist.name: Othar Turner
artist.corridor_connection: Born in Canton, MS and based in Gravel Springs (Como), MS, Turner was the last great practitioner of North Mississippi fife-and-drum blues, hosting annual picnics at his Como-area homestead that became a cornerstone of the corridor's folk tradition.

origin.bio_short: The last great practitioner of North Mississippi fife-and-drum blues — an African tradition predating the guitar — Turner's annual picnics in Gravel Springs kept a centuries-old tradition visible until his death in 2003, when his granddaughter Shardé Thomas assumed the tradition.
origin.bio_long: Othar Turner was born June 2, 1907, in Canton, Madison County, Mississippi, and spent most of his life in Como (Gravel Springs), Panola County. He was the last great practitioner of North Mississippi fife-and-drum blues — an African tradition predating the guitar. Turner's annual picnics in Gravel Springs attracted ethnomusicologists, documentary filmmakers, and devoted blues fans, and his recordings on Rounder Records kept the tradition visible. When he died February 27, 2003, his granddaughter Shardé Thomas led the fife-and-drum procession at his funeral and has carried the tradition forward with her Rising Star Fife and Drum Band. A Mississippi Blues Trail marker at Como honors his legacy.

work.discography_highlights: Everybody Hollerin' Goat (Rounder Records, 1998).

legacy.home_venue: Gravel Springs picnic grounds, Como MS

origin.bio_short: A Mississippi farmer who recorded only late in his career for Fat Possum, Payton's posthumous album Worried About My Baby (1997) revealed a deep, unmediated hill country blues guitarist. He died May 19, 1997, in Holly Ridge, MS.
origin.bio_long: Asie Payton was born April 12, 1937, in Washington County, Mississippi, and farmed all his life in the Holly Ridge area. He recorded only late in his career for Fat Possum Records, whose founders discovered his deep, unmediated hill country blues style. He died May 19, 1997, in Holly Ridge, Mississippi — before his album Worried About My Baby was released posthumously. His recordings remain among the most raw and beautiful in the Fat Possum catalog, a testament to the unbroken hill country tradition in North Mississippi.

origin.bio_long: Paul 'Wine' Jones was born July 1, 1946, in Flora, Mississippi, and was based in Jackson, Mississippi. He was one of Fat Possum Records' core Jackson-area artists, bringing an urban Delta edge to the hill country template while recording alongside R.L. Burnside and T-Model Ford. His work helped Fat Possum define the post-hill country blues sound of the 1990s. He died October 9, 2005 in Jackson, Mississippi. No specific family musical legacy has been reported.

origin.bio_short: As frontman of Booker T. & the MGs — Stax's house band — Jones's organ work created the sonic foundation for virtually every Stax and Atlantic soul record of the 1960s. His 'Green Onions' (1962) remains one of the most recognizable instrumental recordings in American music.
origin.bio_long: Booker T. Jones was born November 12, 1944, in Memphis, Tennessee. As the frontman and organist of Booker T. & the MGs — Stax Records' house band — Jones's Hammond B-3 organ work created the sonic foundation for virtually every Stax and Atlantic soul record of the 1960s. 'Green Onions' (1962) remains one of the most recognizable instrumental recordings in American music. He received the Grammy Lifetime Achievement Award in 2007 and was inducted into the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame in 1992 with the MGs. He remains active in recording and occasional touring internationally.

origin.bio_long: Mavis Staples was born July 10, 1939, in Chicago, Illinois, to a family from the Mississippi Delta. She is managed by Coran Capshaw / Red Light Management.

A Hi Records songwriter, he penned 'I Can't Stand the Rain' for Ann Peebles and remained a largely unsung genius of Memphis soul for decades. He emerged as a solo artist in his 70s with critically acclaimed recordings for Fat Possum Records, proving one of Memphis soul's most gifted but overlooked voices had lost none of its power. He was married to Ann Peebles (also a Hi Records/Royal Studio legend). He died in December 2025 at age 83. His legacy is preserved through the Memphis Music Hall of Fame and Soulsville Foundation.

origin.bio_long: Carla Thomas was born December 21, 1942, in Memphis, Tennessee. As the 'Queen of Memphis Soul,' she was Stax Records' first homegrown star, with solo hits including 'Gee Whiz' (1960), 'B-A-B-Y' (1966), and duets with Otis Redding including 'Tramp.' Her warm, gospel-infused Memphis sound defined a generation. She is the daughter of Stax legend Rufus Thomas (d. 2001). She is retired from regular touring but appears at Stax Museum anniversary events and the Lorraine Hotel Motel Museum. The Soulsville Foundation and Stax Museum preserve her legacy.

origin.bio_short: Co-leader of the Memphis Horns with Andrew Love, Jackson's trumpet defined the horn sound of 1960s soul on records by Otis Redding, Elvis Presley, U2, and Peter Gabriel. He died June 21, 2016 in Memphis.
origin.bio_long: Wayne Jackson was born November 24, 1941, in West Memphis, Arkansas. As co-leader (with Andrew Love) of The Memphis Horns, Jackson's trumpet appeared on hundreds of Stax, Hi, and Atlantic sessions — playing on records by Otis Redding, Elvis Presley, Al Green, U2, and Peter Gabriel. The Memphis Horns were the most recorded brass duo in American music history. He was inducted into the Musicians' Hall of Fame and documented in the Encyclopedia of Arkansas. He died June 21, 2016 in Memphis, Tennessee, and was buried at Elmwood Cemetery. The Soulsville Foundation and Memphis Music Hall of Fame preserve his legacy.

origin.bio_short: Hayes and David Porter wrote over 200 songs for Stax including 'Soul Man' and 'Hold On I'm Comin' for Sam and Dave. His Hot Buttered Soul (1969) and Shaft soundtrack (1971 Oscar winner) revolutionized the concept album. He died August 10, 2008.
origin.bio_long: Isaac Hayes was born August 20, 1942, in Covington, Tennessee. Together with David Porter, he wrote over 200 songs for Stax Records — including 'Soul Man' and 'Hold On, I'm Comin'' for Sam and Dave. His Hot Buttered Soul (1969) and Shaft soundtrack (1971, Academy Award winner for Best Original Song) revolutionized the concept album and invented a new mode of Black artistic ambition. He died August 10, 2008 in Memphis, Tennessee. His estate is managed through private trustees, and the Soulsville Foundation (Stax Museum) maintains a key exhibit dedicated to his legacy.

origin.bio_long: David Porter was born in November 1941 in Memphis, Tennessee. As Isaac Hayes's songwriting partner at Stax Records, he co-wrote the foundational Sam and Dave catalog including 'Soul Man' and 'Hold On, I'm Comin'' — among the most essential songs in Memphis soul history. He later became a significant record executive and music industry educator. He remains active in music education and works with the Soulsville Foundation programming in Memphis, where his legacy is honored at the Stax Museum of American Soul Music.

artist.name: Spooner Oldham
artist.corridor_connection: Born in Center Star (Sheffield), AL and recording at FAME Studios in Muscle Shoals — the primary recording ecosystem shaping the corridor's soul-blues output — Oldham's organ defined the Muscle Shoals R&B sound that flowed through the Memphis-NOLA corridor.

origin.bio_short: Oldham's organ and piano on FAME sessions ('When a Man Loves a Woman,' 'Mustang Sally') defined the Muscle Shoals R&B sound. He is active in occasional recording and live appearances.
origin.bio_long: Spooner Oldham was born June 14, 1943, in Center Star (Sheffield), Alabama. His organ and piano on FAME Studios sessions — including 'When a Man Loves a Woman' (Percy Sledge), 'Mustang Sally' (Wilson Pickett), and 'I Never Loved a Man' (Aretha Franklin) — defined the Muscle Shoals R&B sound. Later collaborations with Ry Cooder, Neil Young, and Jackson Browne extended his influence across rock and folk. He remains active in occasional recording and live appearances. He is inducted into the Memphis Music Hall of Fame.

origin.bio_short: As bassist and co-founder of the Swampers at Muscle Shoals Sound Studio, Hood played on seminal recordings by Aretha Franklin, the Rolling Stones, Paul Simon, and Bob Dylan. His son Patterson Hood leads Drive-By Truckers.
origin.bio_long: David Hood was born September 21, 1943, in Sheffield, Alabama. As bassist and co-founder of the Swampers at Muscle Shoals Sound Studio (3614 Jackson Highway, Sheffield, AL, 1969), Hood played on seminal recordings by Aretha Franklin, the Rolling Stones, Paul Simon, Bob Dylan, Rod Stewart, and many others — making Muscle Shoals Sound one of the most recorded rooms in music history. He remains active in occasional recording and appearances. His son Patterson Hood leads Drive-By Truckers, carrying the Muscle Shoals/Southern heritage into contemporary Americana and rock.

origin.bio_long: Will McFarlane was born in 1952 at Coronado Naval Station, California. After six years as Bonnie Raitt's guitarist (1974-1980), he joined the Muscle Shoals Rhythm Section and became the definitive lead guitarist of the post-Swampers era — recording with Etta James, Bobby Blue Bland, Little Milton, and Johnnie Taylor. He was inducted into the Musicians' Hall of Fame in Nashville. He is semi-retired and occasionally records in Muscle Shoals and Nashville.

origin.bio_long: Jimmy Ray Johnson was born February 4, 1943, in Sheffield, Alabama. As co-founder of Muscle Shoals Sound Studio (with Roger Hawkins, Barry Beckett, and David Hood), Johnson's guitar work on sessions with Aretha Franklin, Wilson Pickett, Percy Sledge, and the Rolling Stones produced some of the most commercially successful soul recordings ever made. origin.bio_long: Roger Hawkins was born October 16, 1945, in Mishawaka, Indiana. He co-founded Muscle Shoals Sound Studio and was one of the most recorded studio drummers in history — playing on sessions with Aretha Franklin, Wilson Pickett, Traffic, Paul Simon, Bob Seger, and many others. He was inducted into the Alabama Music Hall of Fame. He died May 20, 2021, in Sheffield, Alabama. The Muscle Shoals Sound Studio museum and Alabama Music Hall of Fame preserve his legacy.

origin.bio_short: In FAME Studios' original house rhythm section, Carrigan played on sessions with Elvis Presley, Roy Orbison, and George Jones before becoming one of Nashville's most in-demand session drummers for 40 years. He died June 22, 2019.
origin.bio_long: Jerry Carrigan was born September 13, 1943, in Florence, Alabama. He was in FAME Studios' original house rhythm section before the Swampers era — playing on sessions with Elvis Presley, Roy Orbison, and George Jones. He then pivoted to become one of Nashville's most in-demand session drummers for 40 years, recording at Bradley's Barn and RCA Studio B. He died June 22, 2019, in Chattanooga, Tennessee. The Alabama Music Hall of Fame and Country Music Hall of Fame and Museum preserve his legacy.

artist.name: Fats Domino
artist.corridor_connection: Born and raised in New Orleans, LA (Lower 9th Ward), Domino's recording career at Cosimo Matassa's J&M Studio in New Orleans established him as the defining commercial voice of NOLA R&B and rock and roll.

origin.bio_short: Domino sold more than 65 million records and was second only to Elvis Presley in total record sales from 1956-1963. His rolling piano triplets on 'Blueberry Hill' and 'I'm Walkin'' made him the most commercially successful pioneer of rock and roll. He died October 24, 2017.
origin.bio_long: Fats Domino was born February 26, 1928, in New Orleans, Louisiana. He sold more than 65 million records and was, from 1956-1963, second only to Elvis Presley in total record sales. His rolling piano triplets and warm Creole delivery on 'Blueberry Hill,' 'Ain't That a Shame,' and 'I'm Walkin'' made him the most commercially successful pioneer of rock and roll. He remained in his Lower 9th Ward compound through Hurricane Katrina (2005) before evacuating. His home at 1208 Fats Domino Avenue (formerly Caffin Ave) is a historic landmark. He died October 24, 2017, in Harvey, Louisiana. The estate is managed privately by his eight children.

artist.name: Allen Toussaint

origin.bio_short: The central creative architect of New Orleans R&B for 50 years — songwriter ('Working in the Coal Mine,' 'Lady Madonna'), producer, and arranger behind hits by Ernie K-Doe, Irma Thomas, Aaron Neville, and Dr. John. He died November 10, 2015, on tour in Madrid.
origin.bio_long: Allen Toussaint was born January 14, 1938, in New Orleans, Louisiana. He was the central creative architect of New Orleans R&B for 50 years — as producer, songwriter ('Working in the Coal Mine,' 'Ride Your Pony,' 'Lady Madonna'), and arranger behind hits by Ernie K-Doe, Irma Thomas, Aaron Neville, Lee Dorsey, and Dr. John. Post-Katrina collaborations with Elvis Costello (The River in Reverse, 2006) introduced him globally. He was inducted into the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame in 1998. He died November 10, 2015, in Madrid, Spain, while on tour. His son Reginald Toussaint manages the estate.

work.discography_highlights: Life, Love and Faith (Reprise, 1972); Motion (Warner Bros., 1978); The River in Reverse (with Elvis Costello, 2006); The Bright Mississippi (Nonesuch, 2009).

origin.bio_short: Dr. John synthesized New Orleans voodoo mysticism, R&B, rock, and jazz into a uniquely theatrical persona. 'Right Place Wrong Time' (1973) was his commercial peak. He was inducted into the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame in 2011. He died June 6, 2019.
origin.bio_long: Dr. John was born November 20, 1941, in New Orleans, Louisiana, as Malcolm John Rebennack Jr. He synthesized New Orleans voodoo mysticism, R&B, rock, and jazz into a uniquely theatrical persona — the Night Tripper. 'Right Place Wrong Time' (1973) was his commercial peak, but his decades-long role as NOLA's most colorful musical ambassador made him irreplaceable. He was inducted into the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame in 2011. He died June 6, 2019, near Lake Pontchartrain, Louisiana. He is survived by daughters who manage his estate privately.

work.discography_highlights: Gris-Gris (Atco, 1968); In the Right Place (Atco, 1973); City That Care Forgot (429 Records, 2008, Grammy winner).

origin.bio_short: K-Doe's Allen Toussaint-produced 'Mother-in-Law' (1961) reached #1 on both R&B and pop charts. His extravagant showmanship and legendary self-promotion made him one of New Orleans's most beloved characters. He died July 5, 2001.
origin.bio_long: Ernie K-Doe was born February 22, 1936, in New Orleans, Louisiana. His Allen Toussaint-produced 'Mother-in-Law' (1961) reached #1 on both R&B and pop charts — making it only the second New Orleans record to top the pop chart. His extravagant showmanship and legendary self-promotion ('I'm cocky, but I'm good') made him one of New Orleans's most beloved characters. After his death on July 5, 2001, his widow Antoinette K-Doe operated the Mother-in-Law Lounge as a museum until her own death in 2009. The legacy is preserved through the Ponderosa Stomp Foundation.

work.discography_highlights: Mother-in-Law (#1 R&B and Pop, Minit Records, 1961); Te-Ta-Te-Ta-Ta (Minit Records, 1961).

origin.bio_long: Irma Thomas was born February 18, 1941, in Ponchatoula, Louisiana. Known as the 'Soul Queen of New Orleans,' her 1964 version of 'Time Is On My Side' (before the Rolling Stones covered it) and her enduring standard 'It's Raining' represent the gold standard of New Orleans soul. She appeared at Jazz Fest 2024 with the Rolling Stones and released Audience with the Queen in 2025. She remains one of the corridor's most vital active artists. Her label is Rounder/Concord Records.

origin.bio_long: Aaron Neville was born January 24, 1941, in New Orleans, Louisiana. He is a co-founding member of the Neville Brothers and a NOLA soul icon. His pure falsetto on 'Tell It Like It Is' (1966) and 'Yellow Moon' (with the Neville Brothers) defined a strand of New Orleans vocal tradition descended from the Mardi Gras Indian and gospel traditions. He won a 2023 Grammy with the Dirty Dozen Brass Band. He is the last surviving Neville Brother (Art d. 2019, Charles d. 2018, Cyril d. 2022). His son Ivan Neville is active in Dumpstaphunk.

origin.bio_short: The Dirty Dozen pioneered the modern New Orleans brass-band revival by incorporating bebop and funk into the traditional second-line format, inspiring every brass band that followed. They won the 2023 Grammy for Best American Roots Performance.
origin.bio_long: The Dirty Dozen Brass Band was founded in 1977 in New Orleans, Louisiana, growing from Danny Barker's youth program at Fairview Baptist Church. They pioneered the modern New Orleans brass-band revival by incorporating bebop and funk into the traditional second-line format, inspiring Rebirth, Hot 8, and the Soul Rebels. They have collaborated with Elvis Costello, Dave Bartholomew, and Aaron Neville. They won the 2023 Grammy for Best American Roots Performance. They remain active and touring internationally.

origin.bio_long: Charley Pride was born March 18, 1934, in Sledge, Quitman County, Mississippi. He broke the color barrier in mainstream country music as its first major African American star, charting 52 Top 10 hits (including 29 #1s) and winning three CMA Awards and the Willie Nelson Lifetime Achievement Award (2020). He was inducted into the Country Music Hall of Fame in 2000. He died December 12, 2020, in Dallas, Texas, from COVID-19 complications. He is survived by his widow Rozene Pride and three children (Kraig, Dion, Angela). His estate is managed through family and management, with recording rights at RCA Nashville/Sony Legacy.

artist.corridor_connection: Born in Friars Point, Coahoma County, MS — on the Mississippi Blues Trail — Twitty was a Mississippi-born rockabilly pioneer and country music legend who holds the record for the most #1 country hits in history.

origin.bio_short: Twitty holds the record for the most #1 country hits of any artist in history (55 chart-toppers). origin.bio_long: Conway Twitty was born September 1, 1933, in Friars Point, Coahoma County, Mississippi, as Harold Lloyd Jenkins. He holds the record for the most #1 country hits of any artist in history — 55 chart-toppers. His combination of rockabilly roots (performing as Harold Jenkins in the 1950s) and deeply emotive country balladry made him the dominant male voice in country music from the late 1960s through the 1980s. He died June 5, 1993, in Springfield, Missouri. His estate was subject to extended probate litigation resolved through the 'Conway Twitty Amendment' to Tennessee estate law. Recording rights are held by MCA/Universal and Warner Nashville.

origin.bio_short: Buffett turned a laid-back Gulf Coast persona rooted in Pascagoula, Mobile, and New Orleans into a billion-dollar lifestyle brand ('Margaritaville'), becoming one of the most successful touring artists in American music history. He died September 1, 2023.
origin.bio_long: Jimmy Buffett was born December 25, 1946, in Pascagoula, Mississippi. He turned a laid-back Gulf Coast persona rooted in Pascagoula, Mobile, and New Orleans into a billion-dollar lifestyle brand ('Margaritaville'), becoming one of the most successful touring artists in American music history. His posthumous album Equal Strain on All Parts was released with McCartney and Emmylou Harris. He died September 1, 2023, in Sag Harbor, New York, from Merkel cell carcinoma. His widow Jane Buffett serves as personal representative of the estate. The Coral Reefer Band continues to perform as a memorial tribute act.

work.discography_highlights: Margaritaville (ABC Records, 1977); Changes in Latitudes Changes in Attitudes (ABC, 1977); Son of a Son of a Sailor (ABC, 1978).

artist.corridor_connection: Born in Philadelphia, Neshoba County, MS, Stuart is the foremost active preservationist of traditional country music and is building the Congress of Country Music museum in his Mississippi hometown, directly connecting country music to its corridor roots.

origin.bio_short: Stuart is the foremost active preservationist of traditional country music — a Grammy-winning artist and heir of Lester Flatt and Johnny Cash. He is building the Congress of Country Music museum in Philadelphia, MS, and was inducted into the Country Music Hall of Fame.
origin.bio_long: Marty Stuart was born September 30, 1958, in Philadelphia, Neshoba County, Mississippi. He is the foremost active preservationist of traditional country music — a Grammy-winning artist and heir of Lester Flatt (whose band he joined at age 13) and Johnny Cash (whose band he joined in 1979). He has assembled over 20,000 pieces of country music memorabilia for his Congress of Country Music in Philadelphia, MS (in development). He is a Country Music Hall of Fame inductee. He tours with his Fabulous Superlatives and is married to country artist Connie Smith.

work.discography_highlights: Hillbilly Rock (MCA, 1989); This One's Gonna Hurt You (MCA, 1992); The Pilgrim (MCA, 1999); Way Out West (Superlatone, 2017).

research.next_call_or_email: Contact via martystuart.net or Congress of Country Music (congressofcountrymusic.com) for booking.

artist.name: Tammy Wynette
artist.corridor_connection: Born in Itawamba County, MS, Wynette was the 'First Lady of Country Music' whose Mississippi roots defined her plaintive, hard-earned vocal style and whose 21 #1 hits made her the most commercially successful female country artist of the 20th century.

origin.bio_short: The 'First Lady of Country Music' — Wynette's 'Stand By Your Man' (1968) was the first country recording by a female artist to sell one million copies, and her 21 #1 hits made her the most commercially successful female country artist of the 20th century. She died April 6, 1998.
origin.bio_long: Tammy Wynette was born May 5, 1942, in Itawamba County, Mississippi. Known as the 'First Lady of Country Music,' her 'Stand By Your Man' (1968) was the first country recording by a female artist to sell one million copies. She accumulated 21 #1 hits, making her the most commercially successful female country artist of the 20th century. She is survived by four daughters: Gwen, Jackie, Tina, and Georgette (with George Jones). She died April 6, 1998, in Nashville, Tennessee. She was posthumously inducted into the Country Music Hall of Fame in 1998. Recording rights are held by Epic/Sony Legacy.

work.discography_highlights: Stand By Your Man (Epic, 1968); D-I-V-O-R-C-E (Epic, 1968); 21 #1 country hits.

legacy.estate_contact: Estate through daughters. Country Music Hall of Fame: countrymusichalloffame.org/hall-of-fame/tammy-wynette. Recording rights: Epic/Sony Legacy.

artist.name: Pops Staples
artist.corridor_connection: Born in Winona, MS (near Dockery Farms), Pops Staples connected the Delta blues guitar tradition directly to gospel and civil rights-era soul through the Staple Singers, and his tremolo-inflected lead guitar style on 'I'll Take You There' defines the corridor's gospel-soul sound.

origin.bio_short: Founding patriarch of the Staple Singers and the unifying figure connecting the Delta blues guitar tradition (Dockery Farms, Winona, MS) with gospel and civil rights-era soul — his tremolo-inflected lead guitar on 'I'll Take You There' and 'Respect Yourself' shaped a generation of guitarists and activists.
origin.bio_long: Roebuck 'Pops' Staples was born December 28, 1914, in Winona, Mississippi. He was the founding patriarch of the Staple Singers and the unifying figure connecting the Delta blues guitar tradition (Dockery Farms area, Winona, MS) with gospel and civil rights-era soul. His tremolo-inflected lead guitar on 'I'll Take You There' (1972) and 'Respect Yourself' shaped a generation of guitarists and activists. He died December 19, 2000, in Dalton, Illinois. His daughter Mavis Staples (last surviving Staple Singer) carries the collective legacy. The Staple Singers were inducted into the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame in 1999.

work.discography_highlights: I'll Take You There (Stax, 1972, with Staple Singers); Respect Yourself (Stax, 1971); Father Father (Pointblank, 1994, Grammy winner).

legacy.estate_contact: Estate through Mavis Staples management (Coran Capshaw / Red Light Management): mavisstaples.com. Recording rights: Stax/Concord Music, Epic/Sony.

artist.name: Sam Cooke
artist.corridor_connection: Born in Clarksdale, MS to a Baptist preacher, Cooke became the inventor of soul music through his gospel vocal technique, writing 'A Change Is Gonna Come' as a civil rights anthem from his Clarksdale corridor roots.

origin.bio_short: Born in Clarksdale to a Baptist preacher, Cooke became the inventor of soul music — smoothing gospel vocal technique into secular pop with 'You Send Me' (1957) and writing 'A Change Is Gonna Come' (1964) as a civil rights anthem. He died December 11, 1964.
origin.bio_long: Sam Cooke was born January 22, 1931, in Clarksdale, Mississippi (the family moved to Chicago in 1933). Born to a Baptist preacher, he became the inventor of soul music — smoothing gospel vocal technique into secular pop with 'You Send Me' (1957) and writing 'A Change Is Gonna Come' (1964) as a civil rights anthem. He also founded SAR Records as one of the first Black-owned music companies. He died December 11, 1964, in Los Angeles, California. His granddaughter Nicole Cooke-Johnson is a legacy ambassador active in Clarksdale. The ABKCO Music estate manages his catalog rights.

work.discography_highlights: You Send Me (Keen Records, 1957); Chain Gang (RCA Victor, 1960); A Change Is Gonna Come (RCA Victor, 1964); Live at the Harlem Square Club 1963 (RCA, released 1985).

legacy.estate_contact: ABKCO Music and Records (primary catalog rights): abkco.com. MS Blues Trail marker (Clarksdale): msbluestrail.org/blues-trail-markers/sam-cooke. Granddaughter Nicole Cooke-Johnson is legacy ambassador in Clarksdale.
